_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Chowan County, NC. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Edenton — colonial capital with exceptional early records.
display_name: Chowan County, North Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

current_courthouse.location: Edenton, NC — East King Street. The 1767 Chowan County Courthouse is the finest example of Georgian public architecture in the South and is a National Historic Landmark. Still in use.

previous_courthouses.location: Edenton — earlier frame structures on or near the current site
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by the 1767 brick courthouse. Records transferred. Edenton served as the colonial capital and precinct courts met from the earliest settlement period.

record_losses.description: Chowan County is one of the best-preserved record counties in North Carolina. The 1767 courthouse survived the American Revolution and the Civil War without significant damage. Union forces occupied Edenton after the Burnside Expedition but did not target the courthouse. Records run continuously from the colonial period — some of the oldest in the state.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Chowan County's record survival is exceptional. If {ancestor_name} lived in or around Edenton, the paper trail may extend back to the 1710s. The absence of records here is almost always a reflection of the person's social position — not destruction.
record_losses.source: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh, 2001.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — among the oldest deed records in North Carolina
deeds.gaps: No known gaps. Chowan precinct deeds from the 1710s survive at the NC State Archives.
deeds.repository: Chowan County Register of Deeds, Edenton; NC State Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com NC land records

wills_estates.completeness: Excellent — will books and estate records survive from the precinct era. Among the oldest in the state.
wills_estates.gaps: Minimal. Some loose estate papers from the 1710s-1720s are fragmentary.
wills_estates.repository: Chowan County Clerk of Court; NC State Archives
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; NC State Archives digital collections

marriages.gaps: Bonds more complete than registers. Formal register begins approximately 1851.
marriages.repository: Chowan County Register of Deeds; NC State Archives microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Excellent — among the oldest surviving court records in NC. Chowan precinct / county court minutes are nearly continuous from 1712.
court_minutes.repository: NC State Archives; Chowan County Clerk of Court
court_minutes.online_access: NC State Archives microfilm; some transcriptions published

vital_records.repository: NC Office of Vital Records; NC State Archives

substitute_sources.source_type: Colonial court records and Council Journals
substitute_sources.description: Edenton was the colonial capital of North Carolina until 1770. Colonial Council Journals and court records document governance, land grants, and legal proceedings in extraordinary detail for the pre-Revolutionary period.
substitute_sources.repository: NC State Archives; Colonial Records of North Carolina (published series)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Exceptional for the colonial period. Chowan/Edenton records document land grants, militia rolls, tax lists, and legal proceedings for the earliest English settlers in the Albemarle. The Colonial Records of North Carolina (Saunders, 10 vols., 1886-1890) are indexed and widely available.
substitute_sources.source: Saunders, William L., ed. The Colonial Records of North Carolina. 10 vols. Raleigh: State of NC, 1886-1890.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790-1940. Chowan County is enumerated from the first federal census.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for family tracking. Slave schedules (1850-1860) document the large enslaved population.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives. Federal Census Schedules, Chowan County, NC.

substitute_sources.description: St. Paul's Episcopal Church, established 1701, is one of the oldest churches in NC. Its records document baptisms, marriages, and burials for Edenton's leading families from the colonial period.
substitute_sources.repository: St. Paul's Church, Edenton; NC State Archives; Diocese of East Carolina archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The richest church records in the Albemarle for the colonial period. St. Paul's records document the planter elite in detail — baptisms that identify parents and godparents, marriages, and burials. Less useful for common families and virtually no coverage of enslaved people by name.
substitute_sources.source: St. Paul's Episcopal Church Records, Edenton, NC. NC State Archives.

narrative_hooks: Chowan County's records reach back to 1712 — to the colony's earliest years — and they survive in the same 1767 courthouse where they were first filed. If {ancestor_name} had any presence in Edenton, the paper trail is as deep as any in North Carolina.

The 1767 Chowan County Courthouse in Edenton is still standing and still in use — the same building where {ancestor_name}'s deed or will was recorded, surviving two wars and 250 years without significant damage.

Edenton was the colonial capital, which means the records here are not just county records — they are colonial government records, documenting land grants, militia service, and court proceedings for the entire Albemarle from the 1710s.
If {ancestor_name} worshipped at St. Paul's in Edenton, the church records may predate any county document — St. Paul's was established in 1701, before Chowan County itself existed.

Chowan County's record survival is so complete that the absence of {ancestor_name} from the records is itself evidence — it likely means the family was too poor, too illiterate, or too recently arrived to generate documents, not that the documents were lost.
source_references: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.
Saunders, William L., ed. The Colonial Records of North Carolina. 10 vols. Raleigh: State of NC, 1886-1890.
St. Paul's Episcopal Church Records, Edenton, NC. NC State Archives.
Haun, Weynette Parks. Chowan County, North Carolina County Court Minutes. Durham, NC: privately published, 1980s.
Parramore, Thomas C. Cradle of the Colony: The History of Chowan County and Edenton, North Carolina. Edenton: Chamber of Commerce, 1967.
